The courageous person is as fearless as humanly possible -- he fears what deserves fear but faces it rightly, as reason directs, for the sake of the noble. Errors include fearing the wrong things, in the wrong way, at the wrong time. True courage means feeling and acting rightly toward fear and confidence, according to circumstance and reason. Since courage is noble, its end is noble: the courageous person endures for nobility's sake.
summary: Aristotle defines the courageous person as one who fears the right things, from the right motive, in the right way, and at the right time -- all guided by reason and aimed at what is noble. Courage is the mean between excess and deficiency in both fear and confidence, and its defining purpose is the noble.
distilled: The courageous person fears and faces the right things, in the right way, at the right time, and for the right reason -- always for the sake of what is noble, which is the defining end of courage.
notes: The 'right things, right way, right time' formula is the standard Aristotelian specification of the mean, applied here specifically to courage (cf. Book II.6, 1106b21-23). -- The identification of nobility (to kalon) as the end of courage -- and of all virtue -- is one of Aristotle's most important claims. -- 'As reason directs' points to the role of practical wisdom (phronesis) in determining the mean, discussed fully in Book VI. -- This passage establishes the paradigm that each virtue aims at the noble, which Aristotle applies throughout his treatment of the individual virtues.

refashioned: Among those who go to excess, the person who exceeds in fearlessness has no name (as we noted earlier, many states of character lack names), but he would be a sort of madman or insensible person if he feared nothing -- neither earthquakes nor waves, as the Celts are reportedly like. The person who exceeds in confidence regarding genuinely terrible things is rash. The rash person is also thought to be boastful and merely a pretender to courage. Just as the courageous person truly is regarding what is terrible, so the rash person wishes to appear -- and so he imitates courage in situations where he can get away with it. Most rash people are therefore a mixture of rashness and cowardice: they display confidence in easy situations but do not hold firm against what is truly terrible. The person who exceeds in fear is a coward: he fears what he should not, in ways he should not, and all similar failings attach to him. He is also deficient in confidence, though his excessive fear in painful situations is what makes him most conspicuous. The coward is a despairing sort of person, for he fears everything. The courageous person, by contrast, has the opposite disposition, for confidence is the mark of a hopeful character. The coward, the rash person, and the courageous person are all concerned with the same objects but are disposed toward them differently: the first two exhibit excess and deficiency, while the third holds the middle and correct position. Rash people are impetuous -- eager for dangers beforehand but shrinking from them in the moment -- while courageous people are calm beforehand but keen in the moment of action.
elegant: The person fearless of everything is a nameless madman -- like the Celts who reportedly fear neither earthquake nor flood. The rash person is overconfident about real dangers, boastful, and a mere pretender to courage who imitates it only when stakes are low. Most rash people are actually rash-coward hybrids: bold in easy situations, fleeing real danger. The coward fears excessively and lacks confidence -- a despairing type who fears everything. The courageous person is hopeful. All three face the same objects differently: excess and deficiency versus the correct mean. The rash are eager before danger but shrink in the moment; the courageous are calm before but resolute during.
summary: Aristotle maps the extremes surrounding courage: excessive fearlessness is a nameless madness, rashness is overconfidence that collapses under real pressure, and cowardice is excessive fear of everything. Most rash people are secretly cowards. The courageous person holds the mean -- calm before danger but resolute in the moment, unlike the rash who are eager before but fail during.
distilled: Courage is the mean between the rash person who boasts before danger but flees in the moment, and the coward who fears everything -- the truly courageous are calm beforehand and resolute when it matters.
notes: The Celts (Keltoi) were known to the Greeks as fearless warriors; the claim that they feared neither earthquake nor waves may come from Ephorus or other ethnographic sources. -- The 'nameless' excess reflects Aristotle's observation (Book II.7) that the language does not always have terms for every point on the mean-excess-deficiency spectrum. -- The psychological insight that rashness often masks cowardice is penetrating: the rash person performs courage without possessing it. -- The temporal analysis (calm before, keen during vs. eager before, shrinking during) provides a behavioral test for distinguishing genuine courage from its imitation.

refashioned: As we have said, courage is a mean regarding things that inspire confidence or fear, in the circumstances described. It chooses and endures because it is noble to do so, or because it is base not to. But dying to escape poverty, love, or anything painful is not the mark of a courageous person but rather of a coward. For it is a kind of softness to flee from what is troublesome, and such a person does not endure death because it is noble but merely to escape evil.
elegant: Courage is a mean regarding fear and confidence: it chooses and endures because doing so is noble, or because not doing so would be base. Dying to escape poverty, heartbreak, or pain is not courage but cowardice -- it is softness to flee from hardship. Such a person endures death not for nobility's sake but merely to escape suffering.
summary: Aristotle insists that courage must be motivated by nobility, not by the desire to escape suffering. Suicide to avoid poverty, heartbreak, or pain is cowardice, not courage, because it reflects flight from hardship rather than the pursuit of what is noble.
distilled: Facing death is courageous only when motivated by the noble; dying to escape pain or hardship is not courage but cowardice -- a flight from what is difficult rather than a pursuit of what is worthy.
notes: This passage contains Aristotle's clearest statement on suicide: dying to escape suffering is cowardice, not courage, because it lacks the right motivation. -- The view that suicide is a form of moral weakness rather than courage contrasts with some Stoic positions that later endorsed rational suicide. -- The emphasis on motivation (for the sake of the noble vs. to escape evil) illustrates Aristotle's consistent view that virtuous action requires the right end. -- This connects to the broader theme that the exercise of virtue is defined by its orientation toward the noble (to kalon).

refashioned:
Courage, then, is something of this nature. But the name is also applied to five other kinds.

First is the courage of the citizen-soldier, which is most similar to true courage. Citizen-soldiers seem to face dangers because of legal penalties, the reproaches they would otherwise suffer, and the honors they win through such action. This is why the peoples who seem bravest are those among whom cowards are held in dishonor and courageous men in honor. This is the kind of courage Homer depicts -- for example, in Diomedes and Hector.
elegant: True courage has five commonly confused counterparts. First and closest is civic courage: citizen-soldiers face danger because of legal penalties, social reproach, and the prospect of honor. The bravest peoples are those who most dishonor cowardice and honor bravery. Homer depicts this civic courage in Diomedes and Hector.
summary: Aristotle begins cataloguing five types of courage that resemble but fall short of true courage. The first and closest is civic courage, where citizen-soldiers face danger because of legal penalties, social shame, and the desire for honor. Homer's portrayal of Diomedes and Hector exemplifies this type.
distilled: Civic courage -- facing danger for honor and to avoid shame and legal penalty -- is the closest imitation of true courage, as Homer's warriors exemplify, but it falls short because its motivation is social rather than intrinsically noble.
notes: This begins Chapter 8's classification of five kinds of apparent courage. -- Diomedes and Hector are major warriors in Homer's Iliad: Diomedes is the greatest Greek fighter after Achilles, and Hector is Troy's champion. -- The civic courage model reflects the Greek polis ideal where military service was central to citizenship. -- The distinction between true courage (motivated by the noble) and civic courage (motivated by honor and shame) illustrates Aristotle's insistence on internal motivation for genuine virtue.

refashioned: "Polydamas will be the first to heap reproach upon me"; and "One day among the Trojans, Hector shall make his boast..."
elegant: Homer's Hector fears Polydamas's reproach; Diomedes fears Hector's boasting -- both are motivated by the prospect of social shame rather than intrinsic nobility.
summary: Aristotle quotes Homer to illustrate civic courage: Hector and Diomedes are motivated by fear of social reproach and rivals' boasts rather than by the intrinsic nobility of courageous action.
distilled: Homer's heroes exemplify civic courage through their concern with reputation -- Hector fears Polydamas's reproach, illustrating that social shame, not pure nobility, drives their bravery.
notes: The first quotation is from Iliad XXII.100, where Hector contemplates facing Achilles and fears Polydamas (a Trojan counselor who had advised retreat) will reproach him for ignoring wise counsel. -- The second quotation is from Iliad VIII.148-149, referring to Diomedes' fear that Hector will boast of having routed him. -- These quotations illustrate Aristotle's extensive use of Homer as moral evidence, treating the epics as repositories of ethical insight about Greek values.

refashioned: "Afraid was the son of Tydeus, and fled from my face." This kind of courage is most similar to the kind described earlier, because it stems from virtue -- specifically from a sense of shame and a desire for a noble object (honor) combined with avoidance of disgrace, which is ignoble. In the same class one might rank those who are compelled by their commanders, though they are inferior: they act not from shame but from fear, and they seek to avoid not what is disgraceful but what is painful. Their masters compel them, as Hector does...
elegant: Civic courage most resembles true courage because it springs from shame and the desire for honor -- virtuous motives, though not the highest. Inferior to this are soldiers compelled by their commanders: they act from fear of punishment rather than shame, avoiding pain rather than disgrace.
summary: Civic courage most closely resembles true courage because it originates in shame and the desire for honor. A lower form involves soldiers compelled by commanders, who act from fear of punishment rather than a sense of honor -- avoiding pain rather than disgrace.
distilled: Civic courage most closely resembles true virtue because it springs from shame and love of honor, while compelled obedience is inferior since it substitutes fear of pain for fear of disgrace.
notes: The quotation is from Iliad VIII.148, continuing the Diomedes passage. -- 'Son of Tydeus' (Tydeides) is the patronymic for Diomedes. -- The hierarchical ranking of motivations (nobility > honor/shame > fear of punishment) is characteristic of Aristotle's method of ordering apparent virtues by proximity to the genuine article. -- The distinction between acting from shame and acting from fear anticipates the discussion of shame (aidos) in Book IV.9.

refashioned: "But if I catch any man cowering far from the fight, he will have no hope of escaping the dogs."
elegant: Hector's threat -- that cowards will be left to the dogs -- exemplifies compulsion through fear of punishment rather than inspiration through honor.
summary: This Homeric quotation illustrates how military commanders use threats of punishment to compel soldiers, which Aristotle distinguishes from true courage and even from civic courage motivated by honor.
distilled: Hector's threat to feed cowards to the dogs exemplifies courage by compulsion -- the lowest form, driven by fear of punishment rather than any sense of honor or nobility.
notes: The quotation is from Iliad II.391-393, where Hector (or in some readings, Agamemnon) threatens soldiers who shirk battle. -- The threat of being left unburied and devoured by dogs was among the most terrible in Greek culture, where proper burial was essential for the soul's rest. -- This illustrates the lowest grade of apparent courage: pure compulsion, lacking any internal motivation toward the noble.

refashioned:
Those who station troops and beat them if they retreat do the same thing, as do those who draw them up with trenches or similar obstacles behind them: all apply compulsion. But one ought to be courageous not under compulsion but because it is noble to be so.

Second, experience with particular situations is also thought to be courage. This is why Socrates believed courage was knowledge. Different people display this quality in different contexts, and professional soldiers display it in the dangers of war. For there seem to be many false alarms in war, and professionals have the most comprehensive experience of these. They therefore appear courageous because others do not know the true nature of the situation. Moreover, their experience makes them most effective in attack and defense, since they can use their weapons skillfully and possess the best equipment for both purposes. They fight like armed men against unarmed, or trained athletes against amateurs -- in such contests it is not the bravest who fight best, but the strongest and best-conditioned. Professional soldiers become cowards, however, when the danger becomes too great and they are outnumbered or outequipped. They are the first to flee, while citizen-forces die at their posts -- as actually happened at the battle of the temple of Hermes. For the citizens, flight is disgraceful and death is preferable to survival on those terms; while the professionals faced the danger initially on the assumption they were stronger, and when they learn the true situation they flee, fearing death more than disgrace. The truly courageous person is not like this.
elegant:
Those who use trenches and beatings to prevent retreat apply compulsion, not courage. One ought to be brave because it is noble, not from force.

The second type is professional-soldier courage, based on experience. Socrates identified courage with knowledge for this reason. Professionals seem brave because they recognize false alarms others mistake for real danger, and fight with superior skill and equipment. But when outnumbered or truly threatened, they flee first -- while citizen-soldiers die at their posts, as at the temple of Hermes. Professionals fear death more than disgrace; true courage is the opposite.
summary: Aristotle identifies professional military experience as the second type of apparent courage. Professional soldiers seem brave because their experience lets them recognize false alarms and fight effectively. But when genuinely overmatched, they flee -- valuing survival over honor -- while citizen-soldiers die at their posts. This demonstrates that experience-based confidence is not true courage, which Socrates wrongly identified with knowledge.
distilled: Professional soldiers' courage is based on experience and skill rather than nobility of character, which is why they flee when truly overmatched while citizen-soldiers -- motivated by honor -- die at their posts.
notes: Socrates' identification of courage with knowledge appears in Plato's Protagoras (349e-360e) and Laches (194d-199e). Aristotle is partly criticizing this intellectualist view. -- The battle at the temple of Hermes is a historical event, possibly the Battle of Coronea (353 BC) or another engagement, though the exact reference is debated by scholars. -- The contrast between professional soldiers (who flee) and citizen-soldiers (who die) reflects actual Greek military history, where mercenary forces sometimes proved unreliable compared to citizen militias fighting for their own city. -- This analysis anticipates modern discussions of professional versus conscript armies and the different motivational structures each involves.

refashioned: Third, spirited passion is also sometimes counted as courage. Those who act from passion -- like wild animals rushing at those who have wounded them -- are thought to be courageous, because genuinely courageous people are also spirited. Passion above all things is eager to rush at danger, and hence Homer's phrases: 'put strength into his passion,' 'aroused their spirit and passion,' 'hard he breathed, panting,' and 'his blood boiled.' All such expressions seem to indicate the stirring and onset of spirited passion. Now truly courageous people act for the sake of what is noble, with passion as an aid. But wild animals act under the influence of pain: they attack because they have been wounded or are afraid -- since if they are in a forest, they do not approach people. Animals are therefore not courageous simply because, driven by pain and passion, they rush at danger without foreseeing any of the perils. By that reasoning, even donkeys would be courageous when hungry, since blows will not drive them from their food; and sexual desire makes adulterers do many daring things. Creatures driven to danger by pain or passion are not truly courageous. The 'courage' that comes from passion seems the most natural and may become genuine courage if deliberate choice and the right purpose are added to it.
elegant: Third, spirited passion is mistaken for courage. Those who fight from passion, like wounded animals, seem brave because courageous people are also spirited. But the truly courageous act for the noble, aided by passion; animals act from pain, attacking when wounded or frightened but not approaching danger unprovoked. If passion alone were courage, hungry donkeys and reckless adulterers would be brave. Passion-based courage is the most natural form and can become genuine courage when deliberate choice and noble purpose are added.
summary: The third type of apparent courage comes from spirited passion. While genuine courage is aided by passion, passion alone does not constitute courage -- animals fight from pain, not nobility, and if passion sufficed, even donkeys and adulterers would count as brave. However, passion-based courage is the most natural starting point and can become genuine if deliberate choice and noble motivation are added.
distilled: Spirited passion is the most natural raw material for courage, but it becomes genuine only when deliberate choice and noble purpose transform blind impulse into principled action.
notes: The Homeric quotations are drawn from various passages in the Iliad describing battle fury (e.g., Iliad V.470, XI.11, XVI.529). -- The thumos (spirited passion) discussed here connects to Plato's tripartite psychology in the Republic, where thumos is the spirited part of the soul that allies with reason. -- The donkey example is humorously deflating: if fearless behavior sufficed for courage, absurd cases would qualify. -- The claim that passion-based courage is 'most natural' and can be perfected by adding choice (prohairesis) reflects Aristotle's general view that virtue builds on natural capacities through habituation and reason. -- This type most closely approaches true courage because passion is a natural ally of courageous action.

refashioned:
People, like animals, feel pain when angry and pleasure when exacting revenge. Those who fight for these reasons, however, are pugnacious rather than courageous: they do not act for the sake of the noble or as reason directs, but from strength of feeling. Still, they possess something akin to courage.

Fourth, sanguine or optimistic people are not genuinely courageous either. They are confident in danger only because they have won often and against many opponents. They closely resemble courageous people because both are confident, but the courageous are confident for the reasons stated earlier, while the sanguine are confident because they believe they are the strongest and will suffer nothing. (Drunk people also behave this way, becoming temporarily sanguine.) When their ventures fail, however, they run away -- whereas the mark of a truly courageous person is to face things that are, and seem, terrible because it is noble to do so and disgraceful not to. This is also why it is thought the mark of a braver person to be fearless and composed during sudden alarms rather than anticipated ones: such reactions must come more from a settled state of character, since they involve less calculation and preparation. Anticipated dangers may be faced through planning and reasoning, but sudden actions reveal one's true character.
elegant:
People fight from anger for the pleasure of revenge, but this is pugnacity, not courage -- it lacks noble purpose and rational guidance, though it resembles courage.

Fourth, the sanguine are confident from past success, not from noble purpose. Like drunks, they feel invincible -- until they fail, when they flee. True courage faces the terrible because it is noble. Sudden alarms are a better test of courage than foreseen dangers: composure without time for calculation reveals settled character rather than mere preparation.
summary: Aristotle distinguishes pugnacity (fighting from anger for revenge) from courage, then examines sanguine optimism as a fourth type of apparent courage. The sanguine are confident from past victories but flee when things go wrong, unlike the truly courageous who endure for nobility's sake. Aristotle adds that response to sudden danger is a better measure of true courage than response to anticipated danger, since it reveals settled character rather than calculated preparation.
distilled: True courage is revealed most clearly in sudden alarms, which test settled character rather than calculation, while optimistic confidence based on past success collapses the moment fortune turns.
notes: The insight about sudden vs. anticipated danger as a test of character is psychologically acute and connects to Aristotle's emphasis on hexis (settled disposition) as the basis of virtue. -- The comparison of the sanguine to drunken people deflates optimistic confidence by showing it depends on circumstances rather than character. -- The distinction between pugnacity (machimos) and courage (andreios) clarifies that emotional intensity without rational direction does not constitute virtue. -- This passage anticipates modern psychological research on the distinction between trait-level courage (character) and state-level courage (situational confidence).

refashioned:
Fifth, people who are ignorant of the danger also appear courageous. They are not far removed from the sanguine, but are inferior in that they lack self-confidence. The sanguine hold their ground for a time because of their confidence, but those who have been deceived about the facts flee as soon as they discover or suspect the situation is different from what they supposed -- as happened to the Argives when they encountered the Spartans and mistook them for Sicyonians.

We have now described the character of genuinely courageous people and those who are thought to be courageous.
elegant: Fifth, those ignorant of danger appear brave but lack the sanguine person's self-reliance. The sanguine hold ground from confidence; the ignorant flee upon learning the truth -- as the Argives did when they mistook Spartans for Sicyonians and discovered their error. We have now described true courage and its five imitations.
summary: The fifth type of apparent courage belongs to those ignorant of the danger, who seem brave only because they do not know what they face. Unlike the sanguine who have some self-confidence, the ignorant flee immediately upon discovering the true situation. Aristotle concludes his catalogue of five types of apparent courage.
distilled: Ignorance of danger produces the appearance of courage that vanishes the instant reality becomes clear, as when the Argives mistook Spartans for weaker opponents and fled upon recognizing their error.
notes: The Argive/Spartan incident is a historical reference, though the exact battle is debated -- it may refer to an engagement during the conflicts between Argos and Sparta in the Peloponnese. Sicyon was a smaller, less formidable city-state near Corinth. -- The five types of apparent courage (civic, experience-based, passionate, sanguine, ignorant) form a systematic taxonomy ordered by decreasing resemblance to true courage. -- This classification method -- identifying the genuine form and then cataloguing imitations -- is typical of Aristotle's approach to defining virtues throughout the Ethics. -- The ignorance-based 'courage' is the weakest form because it has no internal basis whatsoever.

refashioned: Though courage is concerned with both feelings of confidence and feelings of fear, it is not concerned with both equally: it is more concerned with things that inspire fear. For the person who remains undisturbed in the face of fearful things and bears himself appropriately toward them is more truly courageous than the person who does so regarding things that inspire confidence. It is for facing what is painful, then, as has been said, that people are called courageous. Hence courage involves pain and is justly praised, for it is harder to face what is painful than to abstain from what is pleasant.
elegant: Courage is more about facing fear than about feeling confident. The person undisturbed before frightening things is more truly courageous than the one confident about safe prospects. Courage is defined by facing pain, which is harder than abstaining from pleasure -- and is therefore justly praised.
summary: Aristotle argues that courage is defined more by one's relationship to fear than to confidence. It is primarily about enduring what is painful and frightening, which is harder than merely abstaining from pleasure. This is why courage deserves praise.
distilled: Courage is fundamentally about facing what is fearful and painful rather than feeling confident about what is safe -- enduring pain is harder than resisting pleasure, making courage justly praiseworthy.
notes: This clarification has implications for the relative difficulty of the virtues: courage (facing pain) is harder than temperance (resisting pleasure), which Aristotle explores in Chapter 12. -- The asymmetry between fear and confidence maps onto the general observation in Book II that it is harder to hit the mean from the side of deficiency (cowardice) because fear is more powerful than confidence. -- This passage connects to the broader theme that the exercise of virtue is not always pleasant, developed further in the next section.

refashioned:
Yet the goal that courage sets before itself would seem to be pleasant, though it is concealed by the surrounding circumstances. This also happens in athletic contests: the goal boxers aim at -- the crown and the honors -- is pleasant, but the blows they endure are distressing to flesh and blood, and painful, as is their entire exertion. Because the blows and exertions are many while the prize is comparatively small, the experience seems to contain nothing pleasant at all. If the case of courage is similar, then death and wounds will be painful to the courageous person and against his will, but he will face them because it is noble to do so or base not to. And the more fully he possesses virtue and the happier he is, the more he will be pained at the thought of death -- for life is most worth living for such a person, and he is knowingly giving up the greatest goods. This is painful. But he is nonetheless courageous, and perhaps all the more so, because he chooses noble deeds of war at that cost. It is not the case, then, that the exercise of every virtue is pleasant, except insofar as it achieves its end. But it is quite possible that the best soldiers are not people of this sort but those who are less courageous yet have no other good, for these are ready to face danger and sell their lives for small gains.

So much for courage; it is not difficult to grasp its nature in outline from what has been said.
elegant: Courage aims at something pleasant -- honor and nobility -- but this is hidden by the surrounding pain, as a boxer's crown is hidden by his bruises. Death and wounds are painful to the courageous person and against his will, yet he faces them for the noble. The more virtuous and happy a person is, the more painful death becomes -- for he loses the most. Yet this makes him more courageous, not less, for he chooses nobility at the highest cost. Not every exercise of virtue is pleasant except in reaching its end. Ironically, the best soldiers may be those with less virtue and less to lose, who sell their lives cheaply.
summary: Aristotle observes that courage's noble end is pleasant but obscured by the pain of its exercise, like a boxer's crown overshadowed by his bruises. Paradoxically, the happier and more virtuous a person is, the more painful death becomes -- since he has more to lose -- yet this makes his courage greater, not lesser. Aristotle adds the bittersweet observation that the best soldiers may actually be those with less to live for.
distilled: The most virtuous person has the most to lose through death, making his courage the most painful and therefore the most admirable -- he chooses nobility at the highest personal cost.
notes: The paradox that the happiest person has the most reason to fear death is one of Aristotle's most profound observations about courage. -- The boxing analogy was apt for a Greek audience, where athletic competition was a central cultural institution. -- The concluding remark about the 'best soldiers' being those with little to lose is strikingly realistic and connects to observations about mercenary armies in Greek military history. -- This passage raises deep questions about the relationship between virtue and happiness (eudaimonia): if the exercise of courage causes the virtuous person the most pain, does this undermine the thesis (Book I) that virtue is essential to happiness? Aristotle addresses this tension without fully resolving it. -- The observation that virtue's exercise is not always pleasant qualifies the claim in Book II that the virtuous person takes pleasure in virtuous action.

refashioned: After courage, let us discuss temperance, for these seem to be the virtues of the non-rational parts of the soul. We have said that temperance is a mean regarding pleasures (it is less concerned with pains, and not in the same way); self-indulgence is also manifested in the same domain. Let us now determine what sort of pleasures these are. We can distinguish between bodily pleasures and those of the soul, such as the love of honor and the love of learning. The lover of honor or learning delights in what he loves, but the body is not affected -- rather, the mind is. People concerned with such pleasures are called neither temperate nor self-indulgent. Nor are those concerned with other non-bodily pleasures: people fond of hearing and telling stories who spend their days on whatever comes along are called gossips, but not self-indulgent. Nor are those who are pained by the loss of money or friends.
elegant: After courage we turn to temperance -- both are virtues of the soul's non-rational parts. Temperance is a mean regarding pleasures, less concerned with pains. We must specify which pleasures. Pleasures of the soul -- love of honor, love of learning -- do not fall under temperance. Those who enjoy stories excessively are gossips, not self-indulgent. Grief over lost money or friends is not the pain temperance addresses. Temperance concerns bodily pleasures specifically.
summary: Aristotle introduces temperance as the second virtue of the non-rational soul and begins narrowing its domain. It concerns pleasures, not pains primarily. Pleasures of the soul -- intellectual, social, or honorific -- fall outside its scope. Temperance deals specifically with bodily pleasures.
distilled: Temperance concerns bodily pleasures specifically, not pleasures of the soul such as intellectual pursuits, love of honor, or social amusements -- these fall outside its domain entirely.
notes: This begins Chapter 10's treatment of temperance (sophrosyne), the second individual virtue examined in Book III. -- The classification of courage and temperance as virtues of the 'non-rational parts' connects to the division of the soul in Book I.13 into rational and non-rational parts, with the latter subdivided into the appetitive and spirited. -- Sophrosyne was one of the cardinal Greek virtues, often translated as 'moderation' or 'self-control,' with a broader cultural resonance than the English 'temperance' conveys. -- The exclusion of intellectual pleasures from temperance's domain is important for Aristotle's hierarchy of goods: the pleasures of contemplation are treated separately in Book X.

refashioned:
Temperance must be concerned with bodily pleasures, but not with all of them. Those who delight in visual objects -- colors, shapes, and paintings -- are called neither temperate nor self-indulgent, yet it seems possible to enjoy even these either appropriately, or excessively, or too little.

The same is true of the objects of hearing: no one calls those who enjoy music or theatrical performances extravagantly self-indulgent, nor those who enjoy them appropriately temperate.
elegant: Temperance concerns bodily pleasures, but not all. Enjoying visual beauty -- colors, shapes, paintings -- or delighting in music and drama, whether moderately or excessively, does not make one temperate or self-indulgent. These pleasures fall outside temperance's scope.
summary: Even among bodily pleasures, temperance does not concern all of them. The pleasures of sight -- enjoying colors, shapes, and art -- and hearing -- enjoying music and performances -- fall outside temperance's domain, regardless of whether one indulges in them moderately or excessively.
distilled: The pleasures of sight and hearing, including art and music, fall outside temperance's concern -- one can enjoy or overindulge in them without being called either temperate or self-indulgent.
notes: The exclusion of aesthetic pleasures (sight and hearing) from temperance's domain is significant: Aristotle regarded sight and hearing as the 'higher' senses, more connected to knowledge and contemplation. -- This classification connects to his aesthetics in the Poetics, where the pleasure of imitation through visual and auditory art is treated as a distinct, intellectually informed form of enjoyment. -- The progressive narrowing of temperance's domain (from all pleasures to bodily pleasures to specific bodily pleasures) is a characteristic Aristotelian analytical method.

refashioned: Nor do we apply these terms to those who delight in smell, except incidentally. We do not call self-indulgent those who delight in the smell of apples or roses or incense, but rather those who delight in the smell of perfumes or fine cuisine. For self-indulgent people enjoy these smells because they remind them of the objects of their appetite. One can also observe other people, when hungry, taking pleasure in the smell of food -- but to delight characteristically in this kind of thing is the mark of the self-indulgent person, for these are objects of appetite for him.
elegant: Smell does not directly concern temperance either, except incidentally. Enjoying the scent of flowers or incense is not self-indulgence, but enjoying the smell of perfumes or gourmet food is -- because these smells evoke the objects of appetite. Hungry people naturally enjoy food aromas, but characteristically delighting in them marks the self-indulgent person.
summary: The sense of smell falls outside temperance's main concern except incidentally: enjoying the smell of flowers is not self-indulgence, but savoring the aroma of fine food or perfumes is, because these smells serve as proxies for appetitive pleasures. The self-indulgent person is characterized by this kind of anticipatory delight.
distilled: Smell concerns temperance only incidentally -- when the pleasure of scent serves as a proxy for appetitive desire, as when the self-indulgent person savors food aromas that anticipate consumption.
notes: The distinction between 'pure' olfactory pleasure (flowers, incense) and appetitive olfactory pleasure (food, perfumes linked to bodily desire) is psychologically astute. -- This analysis foreshadows modern distinctions in psychology between sensory pleasure and anticipatory or conditioned pleasure. -- Aristotle's narrowing of temperance to touch and taste (below) systematically excludes the 'distance senses' (sight, hearing, smell) that are less directly tied to bodily consumption.

refashioned: Nor do animals other than humans experience pleasure through these senses except incidentally. Dogs do not delight in the scent of hares but in eating them -- the scent merely told them the hares were nearby. Nor does the lion delight in the lowing of the ox but in eating it; he perceived by the lowing that it was near, and so appears to delight in the sound. Similarly, he does not delight because he sees 'a stag or a wild goat' but because he is going to make a meal of it. Temperance and self-indulgence, however, concern the kinds of pleasure that animals also share -- pleasures that therefore appear slavish and brutish. These are the pleasures of touch and taste. But even of taste, they appear to make little or no use. For the business of taste is the discrimination of flavors, as practiced by wine-tasters and those who season dishes. But self-indulgent people take little pleasure in making these discriminations -- they take pleasure in the actual consumption, which comes entirely through touch, in the case of food, drink, and sexual intercourse alike. This is why a certain gourmand prayed that his throat might become longer than a crane's, implying that it was the contact he enjoyed. The sense with which self-indulgence is connected, then, is the most widely shared of the senses, and self-indulgence would seem justly a matter of reproach because it attaches to us not as humans but as animals. To delight in such things above all others is brutish. Even among the pleasures of touch, the more refined ones have been excluded -- such as those produced in the gymnasium by rubbing and the resulting warmth. For the contact characteristic of the self-indulgent person does not involve the whole body but only certain parts.
elegant: Animals experience smell, hearing, and sight only incidentally -- as signals for food, not as pleasures in themselves. The dog delights not in the hare's scent but in eating; the lion not in the ox's lowing but in the meal. Temperance concerns the pleasures we share with animals: touch and taste. But even taste is peripheral -- self-indulgent people care little for discriminating flavors as wine-tasters do. They crave the contact of consumption itself. A gourmand famously wished for a crane's long throat, revealing that touch, not taste, was his real pleasure. Self-indulgence attaches to the most widely shared and lowest sense, making it justly reproachable -- it connects us to our animal nature, not our humanity. Even among touch pleasures, the refined ones (like gymnasium massage) are excluded: self-indulgence involves only certain bodily parts.
summary: Aristotle concludes that temperance concerns the sense of touch above all, not even taste proper. Animals experience smell, sight, and hearing only as signals for consumption, and the pleasures temperance governs are those we share with animals -- making self-indulgence a degradation to the animal level. The gourmand who wished for a crane's throat reveals that consumption's pleasure lies in bodily contact, not flavor discrimination.
distilled: Self-indulgence is ultimately about the sense of touch -- the most animal and widely shared sense -- which is why it is justly reproached as connecting us to our brutish nature rather than our humanity.
notes: The gourmand who wished for a crane's throat is identified in some sources as Philoxenus of Cythera (435-380 BC), a famous dithyrambic poet known for his gluttony, though other ancient sources name Philoxenus of Leucas. -- The quotation 'a stag or a wild goat' is from Homer's Odyssey X.156. -- The reduction of self-indulgence to touch connects to Aristotle's hierarchical ranking of the senses in De Anima, where touch is the most basic and universal sense, shared by all animals. -- The claim that self-indulgence is 'slavish and brutish' reflects Greek aristocratic values but also carries genuine philosophical weight: Aristotle sees the distinctively human as the rational, and pleasures shared with all animals as sub-human. -- The exclusion of gymnasium pleasures (massage, warmth) shows that not all tactile pleasure is self-indulgent -- only that which involves consumption and sexual appetite.

refashioned: Among appetites, some seem to be common to all, while others are peculiar to individuals and acquired. The appetite for food is natural, since everyone who lacks it craves nourishment -- and sometimes both food and drink -- and for sexual love too, as Homer says, if one is young and vigorous. But not everyone craves this or that particular kind of food or love, nor the same things. Such specific craving therefore appears to be our very own, though it does have a natural element -- different things are pleasant to different kinds of people, and some things are more pleasant to everyone than random objects would be. In the case of natural appetites, few people go wrong, and only in one direction: excess. To eat or drink whatever is available until one is stuffed is to exceed the natural amount, since natural appetite is merely the replenishment of a deficiency. Such people are called 'belly-gods,' implying that they fill their stomachs beyond what is right. Only people of an entirely slavish character become like this. But regarding the pleasures peculiar to individuals, many people go wrong in many ways. Those called 'fond of this or that' are so called because they delight either in the wrong things, or more than most people do, or in the wrong way. The self-indulgent exceed in all three respects: they delight in some things they ought not to delight in at all (since those things are hateful), and when they do delight in things one ought to enjoy, they do so more than one should and more than most people do.
elegant: Some appetites are universal (food, drink, sex), others are individual and acquired. In universal appetites, few err and only by excess -- eating and drinking past the point of replenishment. Such 'belly-gods' have slavish characters. But in individual pleasures, many go wrong in many ways: delighting in wrong things, or too much, or wrongly. The self-indulgent exceed in all three -- enjoying what is hateful, or enjoying legitimate pleasures far beyond what is appropriate.
summary: Aristotle distinguishes natural appetites (shared by all) from individual acquired ones. In natural appetites, the only error is excess -- eating or drinking past satiety. In individual pleasures, people err in multiple ways: wrong objects, excessive degree, or wrong manner. The self-indulgent exceed in all three dimensions.
distilled: Natural appetites go wrong only through excess, but in acquired individual pleasures people err in three ways -- wrong objects, excessive degree, and wrong manner -- and the self-indulgent exceed in all three.
notes: The reference to Homer is likely to Iliad XXIV.130 or Odyssey passages about youthful desire. -- The term 'belly-gods' (gastrimargoi) is a vivid Greek expression for gluttons. -- The tripartite classification of excess (wrong object, wrong degree, wrong manner) provides a precise diagnostic for self-indulgence and connects to the general 'right things, right way, right degree' framework of the mean. -- The distinction between natural and acquired appetites anticipates modern discussions of natural versus culturally conditioned desires. -- This analysis connects to Book VII's discussion of bestial and morbid forms of pleasure.

refashioned: Plainly, then, excess regarding pleasures constitutes self-indulgence and is blameworthy. Regarding pains, however, one is not called temperate for facing them or self-indulgent for avoiding them, as with courage. Rather, the self-indulgent person is so called because he is pained more than he ought to be at not getting pleasant things -- his very pain is caused by pleasure. The temperate person is so called because he is not pained at the absence of what is pleasant and at his abstinence from it.
elegant: Self-indulgence is excess in pleasure and is blameworthy. Regarding pain, the parallel with courage does not hold: the self-indulgent person is so called because he suffers excessively from the absence of pleasure -- his pain is itself caused by frustrated desire. The temperate person is not pained by abstaining from pleasure.
summary: Self-indulgence is defined by excess in pleasure, not by a relationship to pain analogous to courage. The self-indulgent person is distinguished by being excessively pained when deprived of pleasure -- a pain paradoxically caused by pleasure itself. The temperate person, by contrast, feels no distress at abstaining.
distilled: The self-indulgent person's distinctive mark is excessive pain at the absence of pleasure, while the temperate person abstains without distress -- revealing that self-indulgence is a disorder of desire, not merely of behavior.
notes: The observation that the self-indulgent person's pain is 'caused by pleasure' (the pain of deprivation arising from excessive attachment to pleasure) is psychologically penetrating. -- The asymmetry between courage (primarily about facing pain) and temperance (primarily about moderating pleasure) shows that the virtues have different internal structures despite sharing the general form of a mean. -- This analysis connects to the discussion of pleasure and pain as moral indicators in Book II.3. -- Modern psychology's concept of hedonic adaptation and withdrawal symptoms parallels Aristotle's insight about pleasure-caused pain.

refashioned: The self-indulgent person craves all pleasant things, or the most pleasant, and is led by his appetite to choose these at the cost of everything else. He is therefore pained both when he fails to get them and when he is merely craving them, for appetite involves pain. It seems absurd, though, to be pained for the sake of pleasure. People who fall short regarding pleasures -- who enjoy them less than they should -- are hardly found, for such insensitivity is not human. Even other animals distinguish different kinds of food and enjoy some while refusing others; if there is anyone who finds nothing pleasant and nothing more attractive than anything else, he would be something quite different from a human being. This sort of person has not received a name because he hardly exists. The temperate person occupies a middle position regarding these objects. He neither enjoys the things the self-indulgent person enjoys most -- but rather dislikes them -- nor in general things he should not enjoy, nor anything of this sort to excess. He does not feel pain or craving when they are absent, or does so only moderately -- not more than he should, nor when he should not, and so on. But things that are pleasant and conducive to health or good physical condition, he will desire moderately and as he should, along with other pleasant things, so long as they are not hindrances to health or fitness, contrary to what is noble, or beyond his means. For the person who disregards these conditions loves such pleasures more than they are worth; but the temperate person is not like this -- he is the sort of person that right reason prescribes.
elegant: The self-indulgent person craves the most pleasant things at any cost, suffering both in deprivation and in craving itself. The opposite extreme -- total insensitivity to pleasure -- hardly exists and has no name, being inhuman. The temperate person occupies the mean: he dislikes what the self-indulgent most enjoy, feels no distress at abstinence, and desires health-promoting pleasures moderately. He avoids pleasures that hinder health, violate nobility, or exceed his means. He is the person right reason prescribes.
summary: The self-indulgent person is enslaved by appetite, suffering both when deprived and when merely craving. The opposite extreme -- complete insensitivity to pleasure -- barely exists. The temperate person holds the mean: he desires moderate pleasures conducive to health and nobility, dislikes excess, and feels no distress when abstaining from what he should not have.
distilled: The temperate person desires pleasures moderately, enjoys what promotes health and nobility, dislikes excess, and feels no distress in abstaining -- embodying the standard that right reason prescribes.
notes: The nameless deficiency (complete insensibility to pleasure) illustrates Aristotle's observation from Book II.8 that many extremes lack names, and that the deficiency in pleasure is so rare as to be practically non-existent. -- The criterion of 'right reason' (orthos logos) points forward to Book VI's account of practical wisdom. -- The reference to pleasures being 'beyond his means' introduces an economic dimension to temperance, connecting virtue to the practical circumstances of the agent's life. -- The portrait of the temperate person as one who 'dislikes' excessive pleasures (not merely refrains from them) shows that temperance transforms desire itself, not just behavior -- a key distinction from mere self-control (enkrateia), discussed in Book VII.

refashioned: Self-indulgence is more voluntary than cowardice. For self-indulgence is driven by pleasure, cowardice by pain, and pleasure is something to be chosen while pain is something to be avoided. Pain disrupts and destroys the nature of the person who feels it, while pleasure does nothing of the sort. Self-indulgence is therefore more voluntary, and hence more blameworthy: it is easier to become habituated to pleasurable things, since there are many such things in life and the process of habituation involves no danger -- whereas with terrible things, the reverse is true. Cowardice seems voluntary in a different degree than its particular manifestations: cowardice as a disposition is itself painless, but in particular situations we are so overwhelmed by pain that we even throw down our weapons and disgrace ourselves in other ways. This is why cowardly acts are sometimes thought to be done under compulsion. For the self-indulgent person, it is the reverse: the particular acts are voluntary, since he performs them with craving and desire, but the overall condition is less voluntary -- no one craves to become self-indulgent.
elegant: Self-indulgence is more voluntary than cowardice because it is driven by pleasure (which we choose) rather than pain (which disrupts our nature). It is therefore more blameworthy. Habituation to pleasure is easy and riskless; habituation to danger is not. Cowardice as a disposition is painless, but in the moment, pain overwhelms and drives disgraceful acts that seem almost compulsory. Self-indulgence reverses this: each particular act is fully voluntary (done with craving), but no one deliberately chooses to become self-indulgent.
summary: Aristotle argues that self-indulgence is more voluntary and therefore more blameworthy than cowardice. Pleasure draws us in without disrupting our nature, making habituation easy, while pain overwhelms and can make cowardly acts seem almost compulsory. Paradoxically, each self-indulgent act is fully voluntary, yet no one deliberately chooses the overall condition of self-indulgence.
distilled: Self-indulgence is more voluntary and blameworthy than cowardice because pleasure seduces without disrupting our nature, while pain can overwhelm reason and make cowardly acts seem almost compelled.
notes: The asymmetry between self-indulgence (voluntary in particular acts, less voluntary as a condition) and cowardice (less voluntary in particular acts, but voluntary as a disposition) is a subtle structural observation. -- The claim that 'no one craves to become self-indulgent' echoes the point from 3.24 about the gradual, imperceptible formation of character. -- The comparison of voluntariness between virtues connects to the general discussion of responsibility in Chapter 5. -- This analysis has implications for moral education: since self-indulgence is easier to fall into (pleasant habituation vs. painful), it requires earlier and more sustained preventive training.

refashioned:
The name 'self-indulgence' is also applied to childish faults, since they bear a certain resemblance to what we have been considering. Which is named after which makes no difference to our present purpose; plainly, however, the later is named after the earlier. The metaphorical transfer seems apt: whatever desires what is base and develops quickly ought to be kept in a disciplined condition, and these characteristics belong above all to appetite and to the child. Children live at the beck and call of appetite, and the desire for what is pleasant is strongest in them. If, then, this element is not going to be obedient and subject to the governing principle, it will grow to great excess. For in a being without reason, the desire for pleasure is insatiable, drawing on every source of gratification. The exercise of appetite increases its innate strength, and if appetites are strong and violent, they even drive out the power of rational calculation. Hence appetites should be moderate and few, and should in no way oppose the rational principle -- this is what we call an obedient and disciplined condition. Just as the child should live according to the direction of his tutor, so the appetitive element should live according to rational principle. Hence the appetitive element in a temperate person should harmonize with the rational principle, for the noble is the mark at which both aim. The temperate person craves the things he ought, in the way he ought, and when he ought -- and this is what rational principle directs.

Here we conclude our account of temperance.
elegant: The name 'self-indulgence' aptly transfers from childish faults to adult vice: both involve appetite that desires what is base and grows quickly. Children live by appetite, and the desire for pleasure is strongest in them. Without rational governance, appetite becomes insatiable -- feeding on every gratification, growing stronger with exercise, and eventually overwhelming reason itself. Appetites must therefore be moderate, few, and subordinate to reason -- an obedient, disciplined state. As the child follows the tutor, appetite should follow rational principle. In the temperate person, appetite harmonizes with reason, both aiming at the noble: he craves the right things, in the right way, at the right time.
summary: Aristotle concludes his account of temperance by drawing an analogy between self-indulgent adults and undisciplined children. Appetite, like a child, must be governed by reason; left ungoverned, it becomes insatiable and eventually overwhelms rational calculation. The temperate person achieves harmony between appetite and reason, with both oriented toward the noble.
distilled: Appetite is like a child that must be governed by reason: left undisciplined, it becomes insatiable and overwhelms rational thought, but in the temperate person, appetite harmonizes with reason, both aiming at what is noble.
notes: The analogy between appetite and the child draws on Plato's Republic (especially Book IV's account of the soul's parts) and anticipates Aristotle's own discussion of moral education in Book X.9. -- The claim that appetite 'increases its innate strength' through exercise is an early statement of what psychologists now call reinforcement or habituation effects. -- The child-tutor analogy connects to Greek educational practice (paideia) where the paidagogos (tutor/guardian) guided the child's behavior. -- The concluding formula -- craving the right things, in the right way, at the right time -- parallels the general specification of the mean from Book II.6. -- The harmony between appetite and reason in the temperate person distinguishes temperance (sophrosyne) from mere self-control (enkrateia), where reason wins but appetite still resists. This distinction is fully developed in Book VII. -- 'Here we conclude our account of temperance' marks the end of Book III.
